text: The Stoic {{c1::Dichotomy of Control}} separates the universe into things we can control (our {{c1::choices and judgments}}) and things we cannot (external events, past, others' opinions).
explanation: Epictetus argued that unhappiness comes from trying to control things that are external to us. Focusing only on our internal responses brings peace (ataraxia) and resilience.
usage: If your project deadline is delayed due to an external vendor, you cannot control the vendor (external), but you can control how you respond to the situation and adapt (internal).
spanish: La dicotomía del control estoica separa el universo en cosas que podemos controlar (nuestras elecciones y juicios) y cosas que no.

text: Marcus Aurelius described the {{c1::Inner Citadel}} as a mental fortress where one can retreat to find peace, protected from {{c1::external chaos and emotional turbulence}}.
explanation: The mind is a refuge. External events cannot touch it unless we allow them to by forming negative judgments about them.
usage: When surrounded by stress, noise, or criticism, retreat to your Inner Citadel by reminding yourself that others' opinions do not define your character.
spanish: Marco Aurelio describió la Ciudadela Interior como una fortaleza mental donde uno puede retirarse para encontrar paz, protegido del caos externo.

text: The primary rule of Stoicism, taught by Epictetus, states that we should distinguish between what is up to us and what is not; this is called the {{c1::Dichotomy of Control}}.
explanation: The Dichotomy of Control divides events into internal (our thoughts, reactions, actions) and external (other people, weather, outcomes). Stoics focus energy exclusively on the former, accepting the latter.
usage: To practice the dichotomy of control: when anxious, ask: 'Is this within my control?' If no, practice acceptance.
spanish: La regla principal del estoicismo, enseñada por Epicteto, establece que debemos distinguir entre lo que depende de nosotros y lo que no; esto se llama la dicotomía del control.

text: Marcus Aurelius' Stoic maxim that setbacks should be embraced as opportunities for practice is summarized as {{c1::the obstacle is the way}}.
explanation: This principle states that instead of complaining about obstacles, we should use them to practice virtues (like patience, creativity, or courage). The impediment to action becomes the catalyst for action.
usage: Quote: 'The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way.'
spanish: La máxima estoica de Marco Aurelio de que los contratiempos deben adoptarse como oportunidades para la práctica se resume como el obstáculo es el camino (the obstacle is the way).

text: Stoicism defines the only true good as virtue, which is split into four {{c1::cardinal virtues}}: Wisdom, Courage, Justice, and Temperance.
explanation: These four virtues guide all ethical behavior. Wisdom is knowing what is good/bad; Courage is facing fear; Justice is treating others fairly; Temperance is practicing self-control.
usage: Every action must be evaluated against the four cardinal virtues.
spanish: El estoicismo define el único bien verdadero como la virtud, que se divide en cuatro virtudes cardinales: Sabiduría, Coraje, Justicia y Templanza.

text: The Stoic practice of reflecting on one's own mortality to value time and maintain humility is known as {{c1::Memento Mori}}.
explanation: Memento Mori ('Remember you must die') encourages practitioners to live each day deliberately, realizing that life is transient and material status is temporary.
usage: Carry a reminder or meditate on the fact that this day could be your last.
spanish: La práctica estoica de reflexionar sobre la propia mortalidad para valorar el tiempo y mantener la humildad se conoce como Memento Mori.

text: The Stoic attitude of not just accepting but loving everything that happens in one's life is called {{c1::Amor Fati}}.
explanation: Amor Fati ('Love of fate') means embracing all events (successes and tragedies alike) as necessary, useful, and positive elements of your destiny.
usage: When a setback occurs, view it not as a disaster but as a custom challenge designed to help you grow.
spanish: La actitud estoica de no solo aceptar sino amar todo lo que sucede en la vida de uno se llama Amor Fati.

text: To reduce anxiety and prepare for future setbacks, Stoics practice the visualization of potential disasters, known as {{c1::Premeditatio Malorum}}.
explanation: Premeditatio Malorum is the premeditation of evils. By mentally simulating failures (exile, loss of wealth, illness), you reduce their shock value and formulate plans to handle them.
usage: Meditate on everything that could go wrong with a project before launching it.
spanish: Para reducir la ansiedad y prepararse para futuros contratiempos, los estoicos practican la visualización de posibles desastres, conocida como Premeditatio Malorum.

text: The private journal of Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius, containing his personal reflections on Stoic philosophy, was published as the {{c1::Meditations}}.
explanation: Written while on military campaign, the Meditations was never intended for publication. It contains Marcus's internal reminders to practice duty, humility, and reason in the face of absolute power.
usage: The Meditations remains one of the most widely read texts of Stoic philosophy.
spanish: El diario privado del emperador romano Marco Aurelio, que contiene sus reflexiones personales sobre la filosofía estoica, se publicó como Meditaciones (Meditations).

text: Seneca advocated practicing poverty periodically by eating cheap food and wearing rough clothes, a practice known as {{c1::voluntary discomfort}}.
explanation: Voluntary discomfort trains the mind to realize that our fears of loss are exaggerated. By experiencing discomfort voluntarily, we reduce our dependency on luxury and build resilience.
usage: Spend three days sleeping on the floor and eating plain rice to practice voluntary discomfort.
spanish: Séneca abogaba por practicar la pobreza periódicamente comiendo comida barata y vistiendo ropa áspera, una práctica conocida como incomodidad voluntaria.
